title: Advanced OOP and SOLID
summary: Apply SOLID principles to design maintainable, extensible object-oriented systems

sections.title: SOLID as Building Codes for Software
sections.content: Building codes ensure a house is safe, maintainable, and won't collapse when you add a second floor. SOLID is the software equivalent — five principles that prevent your code from turning into a brittle mess as it grows. S (Single Responsibility): each class should have one job, like a chef who only cooks and doesn't also fix the plumbing. O (Open/Closed): classes should be open for extension but closed for modification — you add features by writing new code, not changing old code. L (Liskov Substitution): subclasses should work wherever their parent is used, like a square that's a valid rectangle. I (Interface Segregation): don't force classes to implement methods they don't use. D (Dependency Inversion): depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations.

sections.title: Violating SRP and Fixing It
sections.content: This shows a class doing too much, then being refactored to follow Single Responsibility

sections.title: Dependency Injection and Composition Over Inheritance
sections.content: Dependency Injection (DI) is the practice of providing an object's dependencies from the outside rather than having the object create them itself. Instead of new EmailService() inside a NotificationSender, you pass the EmailService via the constructor. This makes testing trivial (pass a mock) and lets you swap implementations without changing code. Composition over inheritance means building objects by assembling smaller objects rather than extending classes. A Car isn't a VehicleWithEngine — it has an Engine, has Wheels, has a SteeringWheel. Inheritance creates rigid hierarchies (a problem when you later need a ElectricCar that isn't a GasCar), while composition creates flexible building blocks.

sections.title: Deep inheritance hierarchies that violate Liskov Substitution
sections.content: Most common mistake: creating a subclass that overrides methods in ways that break the parent class contract. Classic example: Rectangle has setWidth() and setHeight(), but Square overrides them to keep width == height — violating the principle that code using Rectangle shouldn't need to know it's actually a Square. Fix: prefer composition over inheritance. Wrong: class Square extends Rectangle { void setWidth(int w) { super.setWidth(w); super.setHeight(w); } }. Correct: have Square and Rectangle implement a Shape interface separately.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Refactor a VideoGameStore class that currently does everything: stores inventory in a List, processes sales, prints receipts, saves data to a file, and sends email confirmations. Apply the Single Responsibility and Dependency Inversion principles to split it into focused classes. Describe which classes you'd create and what each one's responsibility would be.
hints: How many distinct responsibilities does the original class have?
Which responsibilities would change if you switched from file storage to a database?
How would you make the receipt printing depend on an interface instead of a concrete class?
